manifest.provider: DWR TRE Altamira InSAR via ArcGIS ImageServer
manifest.study_area: 8 SJV counties (Fresno, Kern, Kings, Madera, Merced, San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Tulare)
manifest.units: feet (vertical displacement; annual layers = displacement during epoch)
manifest.baseline_note: Cumulative layers share reference date 2015-06-13 (Total_Since_20150613). Safe for direct time-slider comparison. Annual rate layers measure displacement during each Dec–Dec period only — not additive across different reference frames.
manifest.slider_recommendation: Use cumulative_layers for sinking time-lapse; annual_rate_layers for year-specific velocity.

explorer_note: SGMA sets basin pumping budgets and GSP rules, but ag production often persists — especially permanent crops (nuts, grapes) that keep irrigating. High ag acreage helps explain continued groundwater stress even after plans are approved. Fallowing is one adjustment margin; consolidation toward larger farms is another.

stats.state_repair_source: SB 872 (2025): up to $150M/yr for canals + $150M/yr for Delta levees through 2046–47

impact_tiles.title: Canals lose slope
impact_tiles.text: Gravity-fed delivery needs more energy and money for the same water volume.

impact_tiles.title: Levees sink
impact_tiles.text: Flood risk rises where channels stay high but banks settle.

impact_tiles.title: Wells fail first
impact_tiles.text: Shallow household wells go dry before deep irrigation wells.

subsidence_callout.headline: Subsidence is largely irreversible.
subsidence_callout.body: Decades of groundwater overdraft lower the water table; as pore-water pressure drops, aquifer clay compacts and land sinks. Sinking can slow, but lost elevation cannot be restored.

subsidence_figure.alt: USGS subsidence benchmarks in the San Joaquin Valley, 1925–1977 and 1988–2016
subsidence_figure.caption: USGS land-subsidence benchmarks — valley ground has sunk tens of feet as aquifers compact.

subsidence_mechanism.alt: Animation: groundwater pumping lowers the water table and compacts aquifer clay, causing subsidence
subsidence_mechanism.caption: Overdraft lowers the water table; pore spaces collapse and land sinks.

stat_boxes.lbl: Peak subsidence rates in hotspots (Faunt et al.)

intro_page.sgma_stakes: The Sustainable Groundwater Management Act requires water managers and farmers to bring overdrafted aquifers into balance by 2040. Studies project up to 1 million acres of San Joaquin Valley farmland may leave irrigated production — a severe economic hit. Pumping limits are already forcing growers to fallow orchards and idle fields ahead of the deadline; fallowed acreage is expected to grow sharply as GSAs enforce budgets (Ag Alert / Maven's Notebook, May 2026).

quotes.text: For all practical purposes, our communities are agriculture, and if we dial back agriculture, we dial back the things we see around us.
quotes.author: Aaron Fukuda, Tulare Irrigation District · SJV Water, Sept 2023

quotes.text: By 2040, overall farm supplies in the valley could drop by as much as 20%—and irrigated cropland by nearly 900,000 acres.
quotes.author: Public Policy Institute of California · Sept 2023

quotes.text:
Farmers face a grim future as pumping allocations are drastically reduced and many must decide how much acreage to retire so they can continue to farm their most productive parcels.

We've got tree guys going after the dairy guys, each one saying the other is using more water. We've got to work together and support each other. This is not helping our cause.
quotes.author: Maven's Notebook · Oct 2025 (Pixley Irrigation District)

glossary.def: A large underground reservoir bounded by rock or clay.
Pumping more water out than is replenished — the valley's chronic condition for decades.
Land sinking as aquifer clay compacts — largely permanent damage. Lost elevation cannot be recovered by refilling the aquifer.
Sustainable Groundwater Management Act (2014) — requires local agencies to balance pumping with recharge by 2040.
Groundwater Sustainability Agency — local public entity with authority to regulate wells and pumping.
Groundwater Sustainability Plan — basin document showing how sustainable yield will be reached.
Water table height vs a pre-2016 baseline. Positive = below baseline (deeper). Negative = above baseline (higher). Not pumping volume.
Irrigated acres left unplanted as pumping limits tighten — growers fallow or remove orchards to comply with SGMA (Ag Alert, May 2026).
Maximum pumping without causing undesirable results (see SMC).

smc.label: Chronic lowering of GWE
smc.desc: Water table must not fall persistently below plan targets.

smc.label: Reduction of storage
smc.desc: Aquifer volume cannot keep shrinking unchecked.

smc.label: Seawater intrusion
smc.desc: Coastal basins must block saltwater moving inland.

smc.label: Degraded water quality
smc.desc: Pumping cannot mobilize contaminants or salinity.

smc.label: Land subsidence
smc.desc: Plans must avoid sinking that damages infrastructure.

smc.label: Interconnected surface water
smc.desc: Depletion cannot unduly reduce streams and rivers.

takeaways_page.lede: Summary of GSP-level changes from 2016 to 2024. Each ratio uses only GSPs with data for that metric — denominators differ because monitoring coverage varies by dataset.

sgma_answer.question: Is SGMA working?
sgma_answer.text: Partly — SGMA is forcing pumping cuts (5 of 5 approved GSPs with fallow data raised idle land), but aquifer and land-surface recovery lag (only 3 of 4 approved GSPs with data show higher water tables in 2024 and 2 of 4 remain below pre-2016 while 45 of 45 GSPs with InSAR data still sank in 2024), so environmental effectiveness is incomplete and compliance costs fall unevenly on producers and communities in the basins still deepest below baseline.

sections.title: Impacts to agriculture
sections.body: SGMA limits pumping; producers respond by fallowing fields and removing irrigated acres. Higher fallow share and lost cropland mean less output and revenue — the main near-term cost borne by growers, not a sign of aquifer recovery.
bullets: Average fallow share across GSPs with data: 10.6% (2016) → 21.9% (2024). Mean change: +11.3 pp.
40 of 44 GSPs with fallow data increased share; 4 decreased.
Total tracked cropland acres: 6,210,495 (2016) → 5,516,282 (2024) — net change -694,213 ac.
31 of 44 GSPs lost cropland acres; 12 gained. 15 GSPs raised fallow while the water table rose 2016→2024; 17 raised fallow while the table fell further.

stats.lbl: GSPs w/ data — lost cropland acres
Approved GSPs w/ data — higher fallow

sections.sgma_takeaway: SGMA primarily hurts producers through idle land: 5 of 5 approved GSPs with fallow data raised fallow share (avg +11.3 pp valley-wide) and 2 of 5 with acreage data lost cropland — pumping limits are paid for in lost production.

sections.title: Impacts to residents
sections.body: Household wells are shallow — they go dry when the water table drops. A higher table 2016→2024 should ease access; a table still below the pre-2016 baseline means deeper groundwater and continued risk. Dry-well report totals also rose as DWR expanded reporting.
bullets: Cumulative dry-well reports summed across GSPs: 76 (2016) → 1,977 (2024).
25 of 45 GSPs with dry-well data report more in 2024 than 2016; 0 report fewer.
Water table (GSPs with data): 17 of 34 higher in 2024 than 2016; 17 lower.
In 2024, 26 of 37 GSPs with baseline data remain below pre-2016 (avg 35.0 ft below); 11 of 37 are above baseline.

stats.lbl: GSPs w/ data — table higher in 2024 vs 2016
GSPs w/ data — above pre-2016 baseline
Approved GSPs w/ data — table higher

sections.sgma_takeaway: Water-table recovery is partial: 3 of 4 approved GSPs with data rose 2016→2024, but 2 of 4 with baseline data still sit below pre-2016 in 2024.

sections.title: Impacts to the environment
sections.body: Land subsidence is aggregated per GSP from DWR InSAR point data (Dec–Dec epochs, feet). Positive values mean net sinking during that year; averages ignore GSP-years without coverage. Explorer maps show the same InSAR source valley-wide.
bullets: 45 GSPs have InSAR point coverage — mean subsidence 0.75 ft/yr averaged across available epochs.
2024 epoch: avg 0.76 ft subsidence among 45 GSPs with data; 45 still sank >0.05 ft that year.
Approved GSPs with data: avg 0.05 ft/yr across epochs; 5 of 5 sank >0.05 ft in 2024.
26 of 37 GSPs with baseline data remain below pre-2016 in 2024 — continued depletion keeps compaction risk alive where monitoring exists.

stats.lbl: Approved GSPs w/ data — sank >0.05 ft

sections.sgma_takeaway: SGMA has not stopped land sinking: 45 of 45 GSPs with InSAR data sank >0.05 ft in 2024, including 5 of 5 approved (avg 0.05 ft/yr across epochs).

sources_page.intro: Data and references for the Sinking Valley Explorer (ECON 30).

items.label: DWR TRE Altamira InSAR — valley subsidence layers
DWR Monitoring Network Management (MNM) — groundwater elevation wells
Land IQ — fallowed land & cropland acres by GSP
USDA NASS — farm size / large-farm share by county
Knight & Lee (2023) — valley subsidence volume estimate
Faunt et al. — peak subsidence rates (USGS SJV study)
PPIC — farm supply & fallowing projections under SGMA
USGS — San Joaquin Valley subsidence photo benchmarks

relationship_variables.label: Water table vs pre-2016 baseline
relationship_variables.good_short: water table higher in 2024 — below diagonal
relationship_variables.bad_short: water table lower in 2024 — above diagonal
relationship_variables.note: Each dot is one GSP. Positive = below pre-2016 baseline (deeper); negative = above (higher). Dashed line = no change. Points below the line = table rose from 2016 to 2024.

relationship_variables.label: Water table trend (4-yr, ft/yr)
relationship_variables.good_short: less falling / more recovery by 2024 — below diagonal
relationship_variables.bad_short: water table falling faster by 2024 — above diagonal
relationship_variables.note: 4-year trend smooths wet-year spikes (2023–24). Positive = water table still falling (not less pumping).
relationship_variables.caveat: Recent wet years can lift short-term trends even while cumulative loss remains.

relationship_variables.label: Fallowed land (%)
relationship_variables.good_short: more fallow with easing GWE stress — SGMA adjustment working
relationship_variables.bad_short: more fallow while GWE loss deepens — painful cutbacks without recovery
relationship_variables.note: SGMA plans use fallowing to cut pumping. Pair with GWE: fallow rising alone is not automatically good or bad.

relationship_variables.label: Dry-well reports (cumulative)
relationship_variables.good_short: fewer dry-well reports where GWE stress is lower
relationship_variables.bad_short: more reports where groundwater remains deeply depleted
relationship_variables.note: Left chart: dry-well reports vs groundwater stress in 2024 (each dot = one GSP). Right: SGMA-era change in report counts.
relationship_variables.caveat: DWR reporting expanded sharply after 2020 — counts reflect both more failures and more reporting. Groundwater context helps separate physical stress from reporting noise.

relationship_variables.label: Cropland acres (Land IQ)
relationship_variables.good_short: stable cropland with easing GWE — productivity without deepening overdraft
relationship_variables.bad_short: stable or rising acres while GWE loss continues — irrigation demand persists
relationship_variables.note: Land IQ total cropland acres — best available proxy; county yield data not linked at GSP scale.

relationship_variables.label: Large-farm share (NASS, %)
relationship_variables.good_short: less large-farm share by 2024 — below diagonal
relationship_variables.bad_short: more large-farm share by 2024 — above diagonal
relationship_variables.note: Share of county farm operations ≥500 ac assigned to GSP — structure, not tonnage.
relationship_variables.caveat: Rising large-farm share does not mean less food production, but it suggests small farms may be losing ground to bigger operators under water stress and SGMA compliance costs.

sgma_window.baseline_note: Pre-2016 = before SGMA enforcement; 2024 = latest data

split_comparison.pre_subtitle: Average groundwater elevation by GSP (ft, MNM wells)
split_comparison.post_subtitle: Same metric — compare left to right on the same color scale
split_comparison.caption: Both maps show monitored groundwater elevation (GWE) averaged across each GSP — not cumulative loss since a baseline. Green = shallower water table · Red = deeper. If 2024 is redder than 2014, the water table fell over the SGMA era.
split_comparison.legend_note: Same colors on both maps. Elevation is from DWR monitoring-well networks — values are comparable within the valley but not raw well depth at a single point.

compare_modes.label: Side-by-side maps
compare_modes.description: Overdraft and equity lens on parallel maps — clearest for comparing patterns.

compare_modes.label: Scatter plot
compare_modes.description: Each GSP as a dot: equity metric vs groundwater stress for the selected year.

compare_modes.label: Stacked overlay
compare_modes.description: Semi-transparent layers on one map (can be hard to read).

overdraft_layers.label: Water table vs pre-2016 baseline
overdraft_layers.description: Same metric as Close view: ft above/below pre-2016 average. Positive = below baseline (deeper); negative = above (higher). Updates with the year slider.

overdraft_layers.label: Water table trend (4-yr, ft/yr)
overdraft_layers.description: Same 4-year linear trend as Close view. Positive = falling · negative = rising. Ending at the selected slider year.

equity_lenses.label: None
equity_lenses.description: Hide equity overlays — subsidence and overdraft only.

equity_lenses.label: GSP status
equity_lenses.description: DWR determination by year. Grey = pre-plan (2016–2019), amber = under review, teal = approved, red = inadequate.

equity_lenses.label: Fallowed land
equity_lenses.description: Idle cropland within each GSP (Land IQ). Rising fallow reflects SGMA pumping cuts and land taken out of irrigated production.

equity_lenses.label: Water access
equity_lenses.description: Cumulative domestic dry-well reports in each GSP through the selected year (2016 onward, bias-adjusted).

equity_lenses.label: Farm consolidation
equity_lenses.description: USDA NASS: share of county farm operations ≥500 ac (2012→2022). Rising share = consolidation toward larger operations.

equity_lenses.label: Cropland acres (Land IQ)
equity_lenses.description: Total cropland acres in GSP (Land IQ). Best available proxy — not yield. High acreage = continued irrigation demand.
